---
title: "Session 12 · Multiple Logistic Regression"
---

## Overview

Having modeled a binary outcome with one predictor, we now bring in several at once. Multiple logistic regression lets each predictor's effect be estimated while holding the others fixed, yielding adjusted odds ratios that separate genuine associations from confounded ones. We close by turning the fitted model outward --- computing predicted probabilities for profiles of interest --- mirroring the move from simple to multiple regression we made in the linear half of the course.

## Learning objectives

By the end of this session, you will be able to:

- Fit a logistic regression with multiple predictors in R.
- Interpret a coefficient as an adjusted log-odds effect, holding the other predictors constant.
- Report and interpret adjusted odds ratios across several predictors.
- Compute predicted probabilities for specified combinations of predictor values.
- Compare nested logistic models to judge whether added predictors improve fit.

## This session's materials

- {{< fa book-open-reader >}} **Read:** [Chapter 10: Multiple Logistic Regression](/notes/chapters/10-multiple-logistic-regression.html){target="_blank"}
- {{< fa laptop-code >}} **Lab:** [Lab 10: Multiple Logistic Regression](/labs/labs/lab10-logistic-extended.html){target="_blank"}
- {{< fa pencil >}} **Homework due:** [Homework 7: Logistic Interpretation](/assignment/hw07.html)
- {{< fa circle-question >}} **Practice:** [Practice Quiz 7](/practice/chapters/quiz7-logistic-odds.html){target="_blank"}
- {{< fa circle-check >}} **Graded quiz:** [Module Quiz 7](/assignment/quizzes.html#quiz-7)
- {{< fa clipboard-list >}} **Project:** [Project 2 — Proposal due](/assignment/project-2-proposal.html)

## Key concepts

| Concept | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| **Multiple logistic regression** | A logistic model relating a binary outcome to two or more predictors. |
| **Adjusted odds ratio** | The odds ratio for one predictor with the other predictors held constant. |
| **Holding constant** | Isolating one predictor's effect by fixing the values of the others. |
| **Confounding** | Distortion of an association by a third variable, which adjustment can help address. |
| **Predicted probability** | The model-implied chance of the event for a given profile of predictor values. |
| **Nested models** | A pair of logistic models in which one is a special case of the other, enabling a likelihood-based comparison. |
| **Linear predictor** | The weighted sum of predictors on the log-odds scale that drives the fitted probability. |
